docs.title: Welcome to Python Programming
docs.text:
Python is a popular programming language used to create websites, games, apps, data tools and artificial intelligence projects. It is known for being clear to read, powerful to use and a great first language for learning how programming works.

As you learn Python, you will practise giving instructions to the computer, storing information, making decisions and solving problems step by step.

docs.title: Python Outputs, Inputs and Variables
docs.text:
Learning Intention Success Criteria
  • Learn how to display outputs in Python.
  • Learn how to collect inputs from the user.
  • Understand how variables store data in a program.
  • Build simple Python programs using outputs, inputs and variables.

By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

  • Use print() to display messages.
  • Use input() to ask the user questions.
  • Store information in variables.
  • Create a simple Python program using inputs and outputs.

docs.title: Today's Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Output Information displayed by a program. Input Information sent from the user to the computer. Variable A named storage place for data in a program. Value The data stored inside a variable.

docs.title: What is Python?
docs.text:
Python is a powerful programming language.

It is used to create:

  • Games
  • Websites
  • Apps
  • Data tools
  • Artificial intelligence programs

Python is popular because it is easier to read than many other programming languages.

In this lesson, you will use Python to:

  • Display outputs using print()
  • Ask the user questions using input()
  • Store answers in variables

docs.title: What is an Output?
docs.text:
An output is information displayed by the computer.

Programs use outputs to show messages, instructions, answers and results.

In Python, we display outputs using print().

Example:

print("Hello World")

This program displays the words:

Hello World

docs.title: Task One
docs.text:
Start at Level 1 and go as far as you can!

Level 1Level 2Level 3Level 4

In Thonny, type the following lines of code:

print("Hello World")
print("My name is ____")
print("I enjoy Computing Science")

Create your own program that displays:

  • Your favourite game
  • Your favourite food
  • A message to your class

Create a program that displays:

  • A title
  • Three different messages
  • A blank line between sections

Create a welcome screen for a game.

Your welcome screen must include:

  • A title
  • Instructions using multiple print() statements

docs.title: What is an Input?
docs.text:
An input is information sent from the user to the computer.

Programs often need information from the user to work properly.

Examples of inputs:

  • Typing on a keyboard
  • Clicking a mouse
  • Speaking into a microphone
  • Tapping a touchscreen

In Python, we ask for inputs using input().

Example:

input("What is your name? ")

This program:

  1. Displays a question.
  2. Waits for the user to type an answer.

docs.title: What is a Variable?
docs.text:
A variable is a named storage place used to store data in a program.

Programs use variables to remember information.

Examples of information a program might store:

  • A player score in a game
  • A username
  • A password
  • A high score

Example:

name = "Mr Wood"

In this program:

Part Meaning name The variable name "Mr Wood" The value being stored

You can think of a variable like a labelled box that stores information.

Variable Name: name

┌────────────┐
│ Mr Wood    │
└────────────┘

The label on the box is the variable name and the information inside the box is the value.

Common Mistakes

  • Forgetting speech marks around text.
  • Misspelling a variable name.
  • Using a different variable name in print() than the one used to store the value.
  • Forgetting brackets after print() or input().

docs.title: Combining Inputs and Variables
docs.text:
Inputs become much more useful when we store them in variables.

Example:

name = input("What is your name? ")
print(name)

What Happens Here?

  1. Python asks the user a question.
  2. The user types an answer.
  3. The answer is stored in the variable called name.
  4. The program displays the stored answer.

Programs become much more useful when we combine:

  • Inputs
  • Variables
  • Outputs

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Create a program that asks for the user's name and displays a greeting.

name = input("What is your name? ")

print("Hello", name)

Step-by-Step

  1. The program asks the user for their name.
  2. The answer is stored in the variable called name.
  3. The program displays a greeting.
  4. The stored name is displayed as part of the output.

docs.title: Task Two
docs.text:
Start at Level 1 and go as far as you can!

Level 1Level 2Level 3Level 4

Write a program that asks the user for their name.

Write a program that asks a user for:

  • Their name
  • Their favourite colour

Write a program that asks a user for:

  • Their name
  • Their favourite colour

Then display both answers.

Create a small interview program.

Ask at least four questions and display all answers.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
Before moving on, make sure you can:

  • Explain the difference between an output, input and variable.
  • Use print() to display information.
  • Use input() to ask a question.
  • Store user input in a variable.
  • Combine inputs, variables and outputs in one program.

docs.title: Python Data Types and Arithmetic
docs.text:
Learning Intention Success Criteria
  • Understand the difference between strings, integers and real numbers.
  • Understand why computers need to know the type of data being stored.
  • Learn how to convert data from one type to another.
  • Use arithmetic operators to perform calculations in Python.

By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

  • Identify whether data is a string, integer or real number.
  • Explain why data types are important.
  • Convert user input into an integer or real number.
  • Create Python programs that perform calculations.
  • Solve programming challenges using arithmetic operators.

docs.title: Today's Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Data Type The kind of data being stored, such as text or a number. String Text that is surrounded by speech marks. Integer A whole number. Float A number that contains a decimal point. Arithmetic Using maths operations such as add, subtract, multiply and divide. Convert Change data from one type into another type.

docs.title: Comments
docs.text:
Comments are notes written by programmers.

Python ignores comments when the program runs.

# This is a comment

Comments can be used to:

  • Explain code.
  • Leave reminders.
  • Make programs easier to understand.

docs.title: What is a Data Type?
docs.text:
A data type tells the computer what kind of information is being stored.

Different types of data are stored and used in different ways.

Data Type Meaning Examples Python Name String Text that must be surrounded by speech marks. name = "Dave" favourite_game = "Minecraft" str Integer A whole number that can be used in calculations. age = 14 score = 250 int Real Number A number that contains a decimal point. height = 1.72 price = 4.99 float

docs.title: Why Are Data Types Important?
docs.text:
Look at the following code:

num1 = input("Enter a number: ")
num2 = input("Enter another number: ")

answer = num1 + num2
print(answer)

If the user enters:

10
20

The output will be:

1020

This happens because Python treats inputs as strings.

Instead of adding the numbers together, it joins the text.

docs.title: Converting Data Types
docs.text:
We can convert data into an integer using int().

num1 = int(input("Enter a number: "))
num2 = int(input("Enter another number: "))

answer = num1 + num2
print(answer)

Output:

30

We can convert data into a real number using float().

temperature = float(input("Enter a temperature: "))

docs.title: Arithmetic Operators
docs.text: Operator Meaning Example + Add 5 + 2 = 7 - Subtract 5 - 2 = 3 * Multiply 5 * 2 = 10 / Divide 5 / 2 = 2.5

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Create a program that asks for two numbers and displays the total.

num1 = int(input("Enter the first number: "))
num2 = int(input("Enter the second number: "))

total = num1 + num2

print("The total is", total)

Step-by-Step

  1. The user enters two numbers.
  2. int() converts the inputs into integers.
  3. The numbers are added together.
  4. The answer is stored in a variable called total.
  5. The result is displayed.

docs.title: Programming Tasks
docs.text:
Start at Level 1 and go as far as you can!

Level 1Level 2Level 3Level 4Challenge

Task 1

Ask the user for two whole numbers.

Add the numbers together and display the answer.

Example output: The total is 15

Task 2

Ask the user for two whole numbers.

Subtract the second number from the first number and display the answer.

Example output: The difference is 4

Task 3

Ask the user for two whole numbers.

Multiply the numbers together and display the answer.

Example output: The product is 24

Task 4

Ask the user for two numbers.

Divide the first number by the second number and display the answer.

Remember: Use float() if the answer might include a decimal.

Task 5

Ask the user for the length and width of a rectangle.

Calculate and display the area.

Formula: Area = Length x Width

Task 6

Ask the user for the price of an item and the quantity bought.

Display the total cost.

Formula: Total cost = Price x Quantity

Task 7

Create a calculator that asks the user for two numbers.

Calculate and display:

  • The total
  • The difference

Task 8

A cinema ticket costs £7.50.

Ask the user how many tickets they would like.

Calculate and display:

  • The ticket total
  • The final cost with a £1.50 booking fee added

A teacher enters three test scores.

Ask the user to enter all three scores.

Calculate and display:

  • Total score
  • Average score

Hint: Average = Total / 3

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
Before moving on, make sure you can:

  • Explain the difference between a string, integer and float.
  • Convert inputs using int() and float().
  • Use arithmetic operators correctly.
  • Complete all Level 4 tasks independently.

docs.title: Python Selection
docs.text:
Learning Intention Success Criteria
  • Remember how inputs, outputs, variables and data types are used in Python.
  • Understand how programs make decisions.
  • Learn how to use selection with if and else.
  • Use comparison operators to test conditions.

By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

  • Write a program that makes a decision.
  • Use if and else correctly.
  • Convert user input into an integer before comparing numbers.
  • Indent code correctly inside selection statements.
  • Use comparison operators such as >, < and ==.

docs.title: Today's Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Selection A control structure that allows a program to make a decision. Condition A test that can be either true or false. If Runs code only when a condition is true. Else Runs code when the if condition is false. Comparison Operator A symbol used to compare two values. Indentation Spaces at the start of a line that show which code belongs inside a statement.

docs.title: What is Selection?
docs.text:
Selection is when a program makes a decision.

Programs often need to choose what to do next based on information they have been given.

Examples of selection:

  • If a player has no lives left, end the game.
  • If a password is correct, allow the user to log in.
  • If a customer is old enough, allow them to buy a ticket.
  • If a score is high enough, display a pass message.

In Python, selection is written using if and else.

docs.title: A Simple If Statement
docs.text:
An if statement checks a condition.

If the condition is true, the indented code runs.

age = 15

if age > 12:
    print("You can watch this film")

What Happens Here?

  1. The variable age stores the value 15.
  2. Python checks if age > 12.
  3. The condition is true.
  4. The message is displayed.

docs.title: If and Else
docs.text:
An else statement gives the program another option.

The else code runs when the if condition is false.

age = 10

if age > 12:
    print("You can watch this film")
else:
    print("You are not old enough")

What Happens Here?

  1. Python checks if age > 12.
  2. The condition is false because 10 is not greater than 12.
  3. The code inside else runs instead.

docs.title: Comparison Operators
docs.text:
Comparison operators are used to compare values.

Operator Meaning Example > Greater than age > 17 < Less than score < 50 == Equal to answer == "yes" >= Greater than or equal to age >= 18 <= Less than or equal to score <= 100 != Not equal to password != "python"

Important

Use one equals sign to store a value:

name = "Alex"

Use two equals signs to compare values:

if name == "Alex":
    print("Welcome Alex")

docs.title: Indentation and Colons
docs.text:
Python uses indentation to show which lines belong inside an if or else statement.

A colon : is needed at the end of the if and else lines.

age = int(input("What is your age? "))

if age > 17:
    print("You are old enough")
else:
    print("You are not old enough")

The indented lines are the instructions that run after the decision has been made.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Create a virtual barman program.

The program should ask the user for their age.

If the user is over 17, the program should display:

What can I get you?

Otherwise, the program should display:

You are not old enough.

age = int(input("What is your age? "))

if age > 17:
    print("What can I get you?")
else:
    print("You are not old enough.")

Step-by-Step

  1. The program asks the user for their age.
  2. int() converts the input into an integer.
  3. Python checks if the age is greater than 17.
  4. If the condition is true, the first message is displayed.
  5. If the condition is false, the else message is displayed.

docs.title: Programming Tasks
docs.text:
Start at Level 1 and go as far as you can!

Level 1Level 2Level 3Level 4Challenge

Task 1

Ask the user for their age.

If their age is greater than 10, display:

You are older than 10.

Task 2

Ask the user for a number.

If the number is greater than 100, display:

That is a big number.

Task 3

Create a virtual barman program.

Ask the user for their age.

If they are over 17, display:

What can I get you?

Otherwise, display:

You are not old enough.

Task 4

Ask the user for a test score.

If the score is greater than or equal to 50, display:

You passed.

Otherwise, display:

Try again next time.

Task 5

Ask the user for a password.

If the password is equal to python, display:

Access granted.

Otherwise, display:

Access denied.

Task 6

Ask the user for their name.

If their name is equal to your name, display:

We have the same name.

Otherwise, display a greeting using their name.

Task 7

Create a cinema age checker.

Ask the user for their age.

If they are greater than or equal to 15, display:

You can watch this film.

Otherwise, display:

You are too young for this film.

Task 8

Ask the user for a number.

If the number is less than 0, display:

This is a negative number.

Otherwise, display:

This is zero or a positive number.

Create a simple quiz question.

Your program should:

  • Ask the user a question.
  • Store their answer in a variable.
  • Use selection to check whether the answer is correct.
  • Display a correct or incorrect message.
  • Use a comment to explain what your if statement does.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
Before moving on, make sure you can:

  • Explain what selection means.
  • Use if and else in a Python program.
  • Convert input into an integer using int().
  • Use comparison operators correctly.
  • Explain why indentation and colons are important in selection.

docs.title: Next Level Python Tasks
docs.text:
These tasks combine the skills from:

  • Outputs, inputs and variables
  • Data types and arithmetic

Important Information before you Begin

You do not need to record your results for these tasks.

This is a test in resilience: try things, fix errors, and keep improving your code.

Remember, we learn through making mistakes.

docs.title: Rules for These Tasks
docs.text:
For every task:

  • Use clear variable names.
  • Ask the user for input where needed.
  • Convert numbers using int() or float().
  • Display answers with helpful messages.
  • Test your program with more than one set of inputs.

Important

These tasks only use the skills from lessons 1 and 2.

You do not need if, else or loops yet.

docs.title: Challenge Tasks
docs.text:
Start where you feel confident, then try the next level.

Level 1Level 2Level 3Level 4Challenge

Task 1: Personal Receipt

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • The name of an item
  • The price of the item

Display a short receipt.

Example output: Alex bought a keyboard for £24.99

Task 2: Minutes Converter

Ask the user to enter a number of hours.

Convert the hours into minutes.

Formula: minutes = hours * 60

Example output: 3 hours is 180 minutes

Task 3: Game Score Total

Ask the user for:

  • Their player name
  • Their score in round 1
  • Their score in round 2
  • Their score in round 3

Calculate and display their total score.

Extension: Also display their average score.

Task 4: Shopping Basket

Ask the user for:

  • The price of item 1
  • The price of item 2
  • The price of item 3

Calculate and display the total cost.

Then add a delivery charge of 2.50.

Display the final amount to pay.

Task 5: Cinema Trip Calculator

A cinema ticket costs 7.50.

Popcorn costs 3.25.

A drink costs 2.10.

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • How many tickets they want
  • How many popcorns they want
  • How many drinks they want

Calculate and display:

  • Ticket total
  • Snack total
  • Final total

Task 6: Room Paint Estimator

Ask the user for:

  • The width of a wall in metres
  • The height of a wall in metres
  • The cost of paint per square metre

Calculate and display:

  • The area of the wall
  • The total paint cost

Formula: area = width * height

Task 7: Fitness Tracker

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Steps walked on Monday
  • Steps walked on Tuesday
  • Steps walked on Wednesday
  • Steps walked on Thursday
  • Steps walked on Friday

Calculate and display:

  • Total steps
  • Average steps per day
  • Total distance walked

Use this estimate:

1 step = 0.0008 kilometres

Task 8: Party Planner

Ask the user for:

  • Number of guests
  • Cost of food per guest
  • Cost of drinks per guest
  • Room hire cost

Calculate and display:

  • Total food cost
  • Total drinks cost
  • Room hire cost
  • Final party cost
  • Cost per guest

Task 9: Mini Budget Planner

Create a program that helps someone plan a weekly budget.

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Weekly income
  • Food cost
  • Travel cost
  • Entertainment cost
  • Savings amount

Calculate and display:

  • Total spending
  • Money left after spending
  • Money left after spending and savings
  • A clear budget summary using the user's name

Extra challenge: Ask for one more spending category of your choice and include it in the calculations.

Task 10: Theme Park Cost Calculator

A group is visiting a theme park.

Ask the user for:

  • Group name
  • Number of child tickets
  • Number of adult tickets
  • Number of meal deals
  • Travel cost

Use these prices:

Item Price Child ticket 14.50 Adult ticket 22.00 Meal deal 6.75

Calculate and display:

  • Child ticket total
  • Adult ticket total
  • Meal deal total
  • Travel cost
  • Final group cost
  • Average cost per person

docs.title: Challenge Tips
docs.text:
• Build one part at a time.
  • Test each calculation before adding the next one.
  • Use float() for prices and measurements.
  • Use int() for whole numbers such as people, tickets or scores.
  • Use comments to explain important parts of your code.

docs.title: Next Level Selection Tasks
docs.text:
These tasks combine the skills from:

  • Outputs, inputs and variables
  • Data types and arithmetic
  • Selection with if and else

Important information before you begin.........

You do not need to record your results for these tasks.

This is a test in resilience: try things, fix errors, and keep improving your code.

Remember, we learn through making mistakes.

docs.title: Rules for These Tasks
docs.text:
For every task:

  • Use clear variable names.
  • Ask the user for input where needed.
  • Convert numbers using int() or float().
  • Use if and else to make decisions.
  • Display clear messages for both possible outcomes.
  • Test your program with values that make each outcome happen.

Important

These tasks use the skills from lessons 1, 2 and 3.

You should use if, else, comparison operators and correct indentation.

docs.title: Challenge Tasks
docs.text:
Start where you feel confident, then try the next level.

Level 1Level 2Level 3Level 4Challenge

Task 1: Age Checker

Ask the user for their age.

If they are 12 or older, display:

You can join the club.

Otherwise, display:

You are not old enough yet.

Task 2: High Score Checker

Ask the user for their game score.

If the score is greater than 1000, display:

High score achieved!

Otherwise, display:

Keep practising.

Task 3: Password Checker

Ask the user to enter a password.

If the password is equal to python, display:

Access granted.

Otherwise, display:

Access denied.

Task 4: Temperature Warning

Ask the user to enter a temperature.

If the temperature is less than 0, display:

Warning: freezing conditions.

Otherwise, display:

Temperature is above freezing.

Task 5: Cinema Ticket Checker

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Their age

If they are 15 or older, display a message saying they can watch the film.

Otherwise, display a message saying they are too young.

Your message should include their name.

Task 6: Test Result

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Their test score
  • The maximum possible score

Calculate their percentage.

If the percentage is greater than or equal to 50, display a pass message.

Otherwise, display a try again message.

Formula: percentage = score / maximum * 100

Task 7: Discount Calculator

Ask the user for:

  • The price of an item
  • Their age

If the user is under 16, they receive a 20% discount.

Otherwise, they pay the full price.

Display:

  • The original price
  • The discount amount
  • The final price

Hint: discount = price * 0.20

Task 8: Delivery Charge

Ask the user for the total cost of their shopping basket.

If the total is greater than or equal to 25, delivery is free.

Otherwise, add a delivery charge of 3.99.

Display:

  • Basket total
  • Delivery cost
  • Final amount to pay

Task 9: Simple Login and Spend Checker

Ask the user for:

  • Their username
  • Their password
  • The amount they want to spend

If the password is equal to python123, display a welcome message using their username.

Otherwise, display an access denied message.

Then create a second decision:

If the amount they want to spend is greater than 50, display:

Manager approval needed.

Otherwise, display:

Purchase approved.

Task 10: Event Ticket Program

Create a program for an event ticket desk.

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Their age
  • How many tickets they want

A ticket costs 8.50.

Calculate the ticket total.

If the user is 13 or older, display:

  • A message saying they can attend
  • Their name
  • Number of tickets
  • Total cost

Otherwise, display a message saying they must attend with an adult.

Extra challenge: Add a booking fee of 1.50 to the total cost.

docs.title: Challenge Tips
docs.text:
• Write the input lines first.
  • Convert numeric inputs before comparing them.
  • Remember that = stores a value and == compares a value.
  • Put a colon : after every if and else.
  • Indent the code that belongs inside each decision.
  • Test each task twice: once for the if outcome and once for the else outcome.

docs.title: Python Programming Assessment
docs.text:
This assessment checks the skills you have learned in the Python basics unit.

You will use:

  • Outputs
  • Inputs
  • Variables
  • Data types
  • Arithmetic
  • Selection with if and else

docs.title: Assessment Levels
docs.text:
Each level becomes more challenging.

Start at the level your teacher tells you to complete.

Level Main Skills Level 1 Read, correct and explain simple Python code. Level 2 Create simple input, storage and output programs. Level 3 Use selection to compare numbers and test scores. Level 4 Build a more challenging program that uses selection and arithmetic.

docs.title: What to Submit
docs.text:
For each program task, submit evidence of your code.

Your teacher may ask you to:

  • Paste your code into your work.
  • Add a screenshot of your code.
  • Add a screenshot of your program running.
  • Explain what your code does.

Screenshot Reminder

Make sure screenshots are cropped and large enough to read clearly.

docs.title: Assessment Tips
docs.text:
• Read each task carefully before writing code.
  • Use clear variable names.
  • Convert number inputs using int() or float().
  • Remember that = stores a value and == compares a value.
  • Test your program with more than one input.
  • Fix errors one at a time.

docs.title: Assessment Level 1
docs.text: Level 1 checks that you can read, correct and explain simple Python code.

docs.title: Task 1: Fix the Input
docs.text:
The code below is meant to ask the user for their name.

name = input("What is your name?)

Write the corrected version.

docs.title: Task 2: Convert a Number
docs.text:
The code below is meant to convert a variable called number into an integer.

number = int(number)

Explain what this line of code does.

docs.title: Task 3: Output a Message
docs.text:
The code below displays a message.

print("Hello World")

Explain what will appear on the screen.

docs.title: Task 4: Read the Selection
docs.text:
Look at the code below.

if mood == "good":
    print("You're happy")
else:
    print("You're not happy")

Answer these questions:

  1. What condition is being checked?
  2. What is displayed if mood is equal to "good"?
  3. What is displayed if mood is not equal to "good"?
  4. Why is the code under if and else indented?

docs.title: Assessment Level 2
docs.text: Level 2 checks that you can use inputs, variables and outputs in short programs.

docs.title: Task 1: Favourite Film
docs.text:
Create a program that:

  • Asks the user to type in their favourite film.
  • Stores the answer in a variable.
  • Displays the film name back to the user in a sentence.

Your output should include:

I'd give it 4 stars

Example:

I watched Inside Out and I'd give it 4 stars.

docs.title: Task 2: Explain the Code
docs.text:
Look at this line of code:

item = input("What is your favourite film? ")

Explain what the line does.

Your answer should mention:

  • The question shown to the user.
  • The user's answer.
  • The variable used to store the answer.

docs.title: Task 3: Happy Checker
docs.text:
Create a program that:

  • Asks the user if they are happy.
  • Tells the user to answer yes or no.
  • Stores their answer in a variable.

If the user enters yes, display:

Glad you are happy!

Otherwise, display:

What's up?

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Submit a screenshot or copy of your code.

Make sure it is cropped and large enough to read.

docs.title: Assessment Level 3
docs.text: Level 3 checks that you can use selection with numbers.

docs.title: Task 1: Largest Number
docs.text:
Create a program that:

  • Asks the user to type in the first number.
  • Asks the user to type in the second number.
  • Stores both numbers in variables.
  • Decides which number is larger.
  • Displays the largest number on the screen.

Remember to convert both inputs into integers.

Example:

Enter the first number: 12
Enter the second number: 7
The largest number is 12

docs.title: Task 2: Test Score
docs.text:
Create a program that:

  • Asks the user to type in their test score out of 100.
  • Stores the score in a variable.
  • Displays YOU PASSED!! if the score is 50 or greater.
  • Displays YOU FAILED!! if the score is less than 50.

Example:

Enter your score: 62
YOU PASSED!!

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Submit a screenshot or copy of your code for each task.

Make sure it is cropped and large enough to read.

docs.title: Hints
docs.text:
• Use int(input(...)) for number inputs.
  • Use > or >= to compare numbers.
  • Use if and else.
  • Check your indentation carefully.

docs.title: Assessment Level 4
docs.text: Level 4 checks that you can combine inputs, variables, number conversion, arithmetic and selection to solve a more challenging problem.

docs.title: Task: Cinema Ticket Price
docs.text:
Create a program that:

  • Asks the user to type in their age.
  • Asks the user to type in the normal ticket price.
  • Converts the age into an integer.
  • Converts the ticket price into a decimal number using float().
  • Gives a child ticket if the age is less than 16.
  • Displays the correct ticket price.

A child ticket costs half of the normal ticket price.

Example:

Enter your age: 12
Enter the normal ticket price: 8
Your ticket price is 4.0

docs.title: Your Program Must
docs.text:
• Use two separate inputs.
  • Store each answer in a variable.
  • Convert the age using int().
  • Convert the ticket price using float().
  • Use selection to check the age.
  • Use arithmetic to calculate the child ticket price.
  • Display the correct ticket price.
  • Work with different ages and ticket prices.

docs.title: Test Data
docs.text:
Test your program with at least three different ages and ticket prices.

Test Age Entered Normal Price Expected Ticket Price 1 12 8 4.0 2 16 8 8.0 3 10 7.5 3.75

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Submit a screenshot or copy of your code.

Make sure it is cropped and large enough to read.

docs.title: Hints
docs.text:
• Solve one comparison at a time.
  • Use < 16 to check for a child ticket.
  • Divide the normal ticket price by 2 to find half price.
  • Test your program after each change.
  • Check that your indentation matches your if and else statements.

docs.title: Python Programming Revision
docs.text:
These revision questions practise the skills from the Python basics unit.

You should only use:

  • print()
  • input()
  • Variables
  • Strings, integers and floats
  • int() and float()
  • Arithmetic operators: +, -, *, /
  • Comments
  • if and else
  • Comparison operators: >, <, ==, >=, <=, !=

Important

Do not use loops, lists, functions, elif or any techniques that have not been taught yet.

docs.title: Revision Rules
docs.text:
For every program:

  • Use clear variable names.
  • Ask the user for input where needed.
  • Convert numbers using int() or float().
  • Display outputs with helpful messages.
  • Use comments to explain important parts of your code.
  • Test your program with more than one set of inputs.

docs.title: Revision Questions
docs.text:
Complete the revision questions below.

Task 1: About Me Card

Ask the user for:

  • Their first name
  • Their favourite school subject
  • Their favourite hobby

Display the answers as a short profile.

Example output: Sam enjoys Art and likes skateboarding.

Task 2: Pet Name Tag

Ask the user for:

  • A pet's name
  • The type of animal
  • The owner's name

Display a name tag message.

Example output: Milo is a cat and belongs to Aisha.

Task 3: Book Page Calculator

Ask the user for:

  • The number of pages they read each day
  • The number of days they read for

Calculate and display the total number of pages read.

Formula: total_pages = pages_per_day * days

Task 4: Water Bottle Tracker

Ask the user for:

  • The size of one water bottle in litres
  • How many bottles they drank

Calculate and display the total litres of water they drank.

Formula: total_litres = bottle_size * number_of_bottles

Task 5: Homework Time

Ask the user for:

  • The number of minutes spent on Maths homework
  • The number of minutes spent on English homework
  • The number of minutes spent on Computing homework

Calculate and display:

  • The total homework time
  • The average time per subject

Formula: average = total / 3

Task 6: Locker Height Checker

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • The height of their bag in centimetres

If the bag height is less than or equal to 45, display a message saying it will fit in the locker.

Otherwise, display a message saying it is too tall for the locker.

Your message should include the user's name.

Task 7: Quiz Point Checker

Ask the user for:

  • Their team name
  • Their quiz points

If the points are greater than or equal to 20, display a message saying the team has reached the target.

Otherwise, display a message saying the team needs more points.

Task 8: Secret Word Check

Ask the user to enter a secret word.

If the word is equal to revision, display:

Correct secret word.

Otherwise, display:

Incorrect secret word.

Task 9: Train Fare Calculator

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Their age
  • How many train journeys they need

One train journey costs 2.80.

Calculate the normal total cost.

If the user is under 16, they pay half price.

Otherwise, they pay the normal total cost.

Display:

  • The user's name
  • Number of journeys
  • Normal total cost
  • Final amount to pay

Hint: half_price = normal_total / 2

Task 10: Plant Growth Check

Ask the user for:

  • The plant name
  • Its starting height in centimetres
  • Its new height in centimetres

Calculate how much the plant has grown.

If the plant has grown more than 5 centimetres, display a message saying it has grown well.

Otherwise, display a message saying it needs more time.

Display the amount grown in your output.

Task 11: School Trip Lunch Order

Ask the user for:

  • The student's name
  • Number of sandwiches
  • Number of fruit bags
  • Number of juice cartons

Use these prices:

Item Price Sandwich 1.80 Fruit bag 0.90 Juice carton 0.75

Calculate and display:

  • Sandwich total
  • Fruit total
  • Juice total
  • Final lunch cost

If the final lunch cost is greater than 5.00, display:

Check this order with a teacher.

Otherwise, display:

Order accepted.

Task 12: Revision Session Planner

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Minutes spent revising Python
  • Minutes spent revising Maths
  • Minutes spent revising English

Calculate and display:

  • Total revision time
  • Average revision time per subject

If the total revision time is greater than or equal to 60, display a message saying they met the revision target.

Otherwise, display a message saying they should plan another short session.

Your final output should include the user's name.

docs.title: Before You Finish
docs.text:
Check that each program:

  • Runs without errors.
  • Uses int() or float() for number inputs.
  • Uses if and else correctly where needed.
  • Has correct indentation after if and else.
  • Gives clear output messages.
